sections.title: Definitions of key personnel and authorities for law enforcement
sections.summary: This section defines specific roles and entities relevant to law enforcement, including chief officers, certifying officials, and members of Commonwealth Royal Commissions. It specifies who qualifies as a chief officer for various agencies, such as the Australian Federal Police, National Anti-Corruption Commission, and state police forces. It also outlines the roles of certifying persons, including Deputy Directors-General of Security and authorized personnel.

requirements.text: Chief officer, in relation to an agency, an eligible Commonwealth authority or an eligible authority of a State, means: (a) in the case of the Australian Federal Police—the Commissioner of Police; or the National Anti-Corruption Commissioner; or (b) in the case of the ACC—the Chief Executive Officer of the ACC; or (ba) in the case of an eligible Commonwealth authority—the member constituting, or the member who generally presides at hearings and other meetings of, the Commonwealth Royal Commission concerned; or (c) in the case of the Police Force of a State—the Commissioner of that Police Force; or (d) in the case of the Crime Commission (NSW)—the Commissioner of the Crime Commission (NSW); or Corruption (NSW)—the Chief Commissioner of the Independent Commission Against Corruption (NSW); or (eb) in the case of the IBAC (Vic.)—the Commissioner of the IBAC (Vic.); or (ec) in the case of the Victorian Inspectorate—the Inspector of the Victorian Inspectorate; or the chairperson of the Crime and Corruption Commission (Qld); or (NSW)—the Chief Commissioner of the Law Enforcement Commission (NSW)—the Inspector of the Law Enforcement Commission (NSW)—the Commissioner of the Corruption and Crime Commission (WA)—the Parliamentary Inspector of the Corruption and Crime Commission (WA); or (la) in the case of the Independent Commission Against Corruption (SA)—the Commissioner of the Independent Commission Against Corruption (SA); or (lb) in the case of the Inspector of the Independent Commission Against Corruption (SA)—the Inspector of the Independent Commission Against Corruption (SA); or (m) in the case of an enforcement agency that is not an interception agency and is not an eligible authority of a State—the chief executive officer or an acting chief executive officer of the agency.
Certifying person means any of the following: (b) a Deputy Director-General of Security; (c) a person authorised to be a certifying person of the Organisation under section 5AD.
Commonwealth agency means: (aa) the National Anti-Corruption Commission; or (b) the ACC.
Commonwealth Royal Commission means a Royal Commission within the meaning of the Royal Commissions Act 1902.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for telecommunications and interception warrants
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in telecommunications and interception warrant contexts. It specifies what constitutes a renewal warrant, renewal application, responsible person for a computer network, restricted record, retained data, satellite-based facility, security authority, and other critical terms. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding telecommunications services, interceptions, and warrant applications under Australian law.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify the scope of telecommunications systems and data that may be subject to interception warrants, which is relevant for determining what data can be transferred or processed across borders under international data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: A renewal warrant means a warrant issued to the agency in respect of that service or person, being an application made while a warrant issued to the agency in respect of that service or person is still in force.

A renewal application means an application by an agency for a warrant in respect of a telecommunications service or person, being an application made while a warrant issued to the agency in respect of that service or person is still in force.
A responsible person for a computer network means the individual who operates the network or the head of the body that operates the network.
A restricted record means a record obtained by means of an interception of a communication passing over a telecommunications system, excluding specific types of intercept information.
Retained data means information or documents that a service provider is or has been required to keep under Part 5-1A.
A satellite-based facility means a facility in a satellite.
A security authority means an authority of the Commonwealth with functions primarily relating to security, foreign intelligence, defence, or international affairs.
A service provider has the meaning given by subsection 187A(1).
A stored communication (except when used in Schedule 1) means a communication held on equipment operated by a carrier that cannot be accessed without assistance from a carrier employee.
A telecommunications system means a network within Australia or partly within Australia, including connected equipment and facilities within Australia.

sections.title: Telecommunications system communication definitions
sections.summary: This section defines when a communication starts and continues passing over a telecommunications system, and who the intended recipient is. It specifies that a communication starts when sent by the sender and continues until accessible to the recipient. The intended recipient is defined as the individual addressed, the person addressed if not an individual, or the person controlling the service if not addressed.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key telecommunications terms relevant to data transfers across borders, including when communication starts/ends and who the recipient is, which impacts cross-border data flow analysis.

requirements.text: A communication starts passing over a telecommunications system when sent or transmitted by the sender
A communication continues passing over the system until accessible to the intended recipient
The intended recipient is the individual addressed if the communication is addressed to an individual
The intended recipient is the person addressed if the communication is not addressed to an individual
The intended recipient is the person controlling the telecommunications service if the communication is not addressed to a person

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Act (other than Schedule 1), a communication: (a) is taken to start passing over a telecommunications system when it is sent or transmitted by the person sending the (b) is taken to continue to pass over the system until it becomes accessible to the intended recipient of the communication.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Telecommunications interception definitions
sections.summary: This section defines when a communication is accessible to its intended recipient and what constitutes interception of a communication. It specifies that interception involves listening or recording communications without the knowledge of the sender. It also outlines exceptions for emergency services, authorized personnel, and specific regulatory activities where listening/recording does not constitute interception.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when communications are intercepted and exceptions for emergency services and authorized personnel, critical for assessing risks in cross-border data transfers involving interception.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Staff member declarations for State Police Forces
sections.summary: This section defines staff members for State Police Forces under the Australian Federal Police Act. It specifies that the Minister may declare certain agency members as staff members corresponding to non-Federal Police employees. These declared members are referred to as staff members in relation to their agency for the purposes of the Act.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibition on interception of telecommunications
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized interception of telecommunications communications. It specifies exceptions for lawful activities such as carrier duties, ASIO operations, warrants, and emergency situations where immediate action is necessary to prevent serious harm. The prohibition applies to telecommunications systems and includes specific conditions for emergency interception without a warrant.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which telecommunications interception is permitted without a warrant, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving cross-border communications. It specifies when interception is allowed for security purposes without requiring a warrant, which impacts data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: A person shall not intercept, authorize, suffer or permit another person to intercept, or do any act or thing that will enable him or her or another person to intercept a communication passing over a telecommunications system
Interception is permitted for carrier duties in the installation, operation, or maintenance of telecommunications systems
Interception is permitted for ASIO employees in lawful performance of duties to discover listening devices
Interception is permitted for ASIO affiliates in accordance with contracts to determine listening device locations
Interception is permitted under warrants issued by the Attorney-General
Interception is permitted for emergency situations where there are reasonable grounds for suspecting imminent serious harm
Interception is permitted when a person consents to the action and there are reasonable grounds for believing imminent serious harm

sections.source_quote: Section 7 (1) A person shall not intercept, authorize, suffer or permit another person to intercept, or do any act or thing that will enable him or her or another person to intercept a communication passing over a telecommunications system. (2) Subsection (1) does not apply to or in relation to: (a) an act or thing done by an employee of a carrier in the course of his or her duties for or in connection with: (i) the installation of any line, or the installation of any equipment, used or intended for use in connection with a telecommunications service; (ii) the operation or maintenance of a telecommunications system; (iii) the identifying or tracing of any person who has contravened, or is suspected of having contravened or being likely to contravene, a provision of Part 10.6 of the Criminal Code; (aa) the interception of a communication by another person lawfully engaged in duties relating to the installation, connection or maintenance of equipment or a line, where it is reasonably necessary for the person to intercept the communication in order to perform those duties effectively; (aaa) the interception of a communication by a person if: (i) the person is authorised, in writing, by a responsible person for a computer network to engage in network protection duties in relation to the network; and (ii) it is reasonably necessary for the person to intercept the communication in order to perform those duties effectively; (ab) the interception of a communication by a person lawfully engaged in duties relating to the installation, connection or maintenance of equipment used, or to be used, for the interception of communications under warrants; (ac) the interception of a communication where the interception results from, or is incidental to, action taken by an ASIO employee, in the lawful performance of his or her duties, for the purpose of: (i) discovering whether a listening device is being used at, or in relation to, a particular place; or (ii) determining the location of a listening device; (ad) the interception of a communication where the interception results from, or is incidental to, action taken by an ASIO affiliate, in accordance with the contract, agreement or other arrangement under which the ASIO affiliate is performing functions or services for the Organisation, for the purpose of: (i) discovering whether a listening device is being used at, or in relation to, a particular place; or (ii) determining the location of a listening device; (b) the interception of a communication under a warrant; (ba) the interception of a communication under subsection 25A(4) or (8), 27A(1) or (3C), 2:27E(2) or 27E(6) of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979; (bb) the interception of a communication under subsection 27E(7), 27KE(9) or 27KP(8) of the Surveillance Devices Act 2004; (bc) an act or thing done in compliance with an international production order (within the meaning of Schedule 1); (c) the interception of a communication pursuant to a request made, or purporting to be made, under subsection 30(1) or (2); (d) the interception of a communication under an authorisation under section 31A.
sections.relevance_reason: Permits lawful interception exceptions

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Telecommunications warrant conditions for foreign intelligence interception
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions for warrants authorizing telecommunications interception to obtain foreign intelligence. The warrant must specify the person or telecommunications service, include grounds for suspicion of security threats, and ensure no other practicable methods exist to identify the target. The warrant must also state the period of validity (up to 6 months) and may authorize entry on premises for equipment installation.
sections.dtia_summary: Warrants for foreign intelligence interception require specific conditions including identification of targets, security grounds, and exclusion of alternative methods. These conditions impact data transfer assessments by mandating precise target identification and limiting interception to foreign communications.

Warrant must include grounds for suspicion of security threats

sections.title: Authorisation of interception for communications by security officers
sections.summary: This section authorises the Director-General of Security or ASIO employees to approve individuals as interceptors for communications under warrants. It specifies that approval extends to new offices created after approval, requires written records of intercepted communications, and mandates discontinuation of interception if warrant grounds cease. The Organisation must maintain records of all warrants and their execution.

sections.title: Emergency interception of communications for urgent cases
sections.summary: This section allows emergency requests for intercepting communications to locate callers in life-threatening situations. It requires police to notify carriers of emergency requests, and carriers to provide location information to police within the emergency context. The process must be documented and limited to specific emergency scenarios.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines emergency interception procedures for locating callers in life-threatening situations, requiring coordination between police and carriers. It impacts data transfer assessments by defining when and how emergency communications can be intercepted without formal warrants.

sections.title: Destruction of records by security authorities
sections.summary: Security authorities must destroy records obtained through telecommunications interception that are no longer needed for development or testing purposes. This applies to information acquired via interception of communications over telecommunications systems. The destruction must occur as soon as practicable after the records are no longer required.

sections.title: State authorities authorized to apply for warrants
sections.summary: State authorities can apply for warrants to intercept telecommunications under specific conditions. These criteria include satisfactory provisions for the chief officer of the authority and inspection requirements.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Application requirements for interception warrants
sections.summary: This section outlines the specific information that must be included in written applications for warrants to intercept telecommunications services or individuals. Applications must state the agency's name and the applicant's name. Affidavits accompanying applications must detail the reasons for the warrant's duration, the specific telecommunications services or individuals involved, and previous warrant applications. For telecommunications services, the affidavit must include historical data on prior applications and warrants, and how the agency has used intercepted information. The section also specifies that multiple affidavits may be used to cover all required information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed requirements for applications to intercept communications, including specific information about telecommunications services or individuals, historical warrant data, and usage of intercepted information. These requirements directly impact data transfer impact assessments by defining the scope and conditions under which interception warrants can be issued, particularly for cross-border data flows involving telecommunications services.

sections.heading: Preliminary  Part 1-1 (cont.)
sections.title: Warrant issuance conditions for telecommunications interception
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions under which a warrant may be issued for telecommunications interception. It specifies that the warrant must be issued by a Judge or nominated ART member after verifying previous applications and warrants, and that the interception must not jeopardize security of an investigation into serious offences. The warrant must detail specific technical reasons or circumstances where interception would be impracticable otherwise. The warrant must also specify the period of validity (up to 45 or 90 days) and include short particulars of serious offences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for telecommunications interception warrants that require technical justification and security assessments, directly impacting cross-border data transfer scenarios involving telecommunications services.

sections.source_quote: Where: (a) an agency applies under this section to an eligible Judge or (b) the Judge or nominated ART member is satisfied that subsection (2) has been complied with in relation to the application, that section 46 would empower the Judge or nominated ART member to issue a warrant if the application had been made under either of those sections; and (ca) Division 3 has been complied with in relation to the application, (d) the Judge or nominated ART member is satisfied, on the basis of the information given to the Judge or nominated ART member under this Part in connection with the application, that: (i) for technical reasons connected with the nature or operation of the service or of a telecommunications system of which the service forms a part; or (ii) where, if the warrant were issued under section 46, communications to or from the telecommunications service would be intercepted while passing over a telecommunications system operated by a carrier—execution of the warrant as a result of action taken by employees of that carrier might jeopardise security of the investigation by the agency of a serious offence in which a person to whom the application relates is involved or, in the case of a warrant issued in the circumstances mentioned in subsection 46(4) or (9), might jeopardise the achievement of an objective for which the warrant was issued

sections.title: Warrant revocation procedures for telecommunications interceptions
sections.summary: This section details procedures for revoking warrants issued for telecommunications interceptions. It requires the chief officer of an agency to revoke warrants if the grounds for issuance cease to exist, and mandates immediate notification to relevant authorities upon revocation. The revocation must be communicated to the Secretary of the Department and other agencies to ensure discontinuation of interceptions. The warrant remains valid until the revocation instrument is received by the Secretary or the warrant expires.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Restrictions on sharing foreign intelligence information with specific entities
sections.summary: Foreign intelligence information cannot be shared with Commonwealth, State, ASIS, ASD, or AGO staff members for purposes outside the development or testing of technologies or interception capabilities unless it was obtained under a section 31A authorization.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts sharing of foreign intelligence information with specific entities; relevant for DTIA due to restrictions on cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: Foreign intelligence information cannot be shared with Commonwealth staff members for purposes outside technology or interception capability development.
Foreign intelligence information cannot be shared with State staff members for purposes outside technology or interception capability development.
Foreign intelligence information cannot be shared with ASIS, ASD, or AGO staff members for purposes outside technology or interception capability development.

sections.source_quote: If lawfully intercepted information was obtained under a section 31A authorisation, subsection (1) of this section does not authorise the communication of the information in accordance with subsection 18(3) of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 to a staff member of an authority of the Commonwealth; unless the communication is for the purpose of the development or testing of technologies, or interception capabilities, of that authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure intelligence sharing restrictions, no data protection relevance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Agency authorizations for lawfully intercepted information
sections.summary: An agency's chief officer or authorising officer may authorize the use of lawfully intercepted information for investigations related to warrants issued to the agency. This authorization is limited to purposes connected with the investigation and applies only to persons not already within the agency.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows agencies to authorize use of intercepted information for specific investigations; relevant for DTIA due to restrictions on cross-border data sharing.

requirements.text: An agency's chief officer or authorising officer may make an authorization for a person not an agency staff member for purposes connected with an investigation.
An agency's chief officer may appoint an authorising officer in writing.

sections.source_quote: The chief officer, or an authorising officer, of an agency may make an authorisation under subsection (2) in relation to a person (or class of person) who is not an officer or staff member of that agency only for a purpose or purposes connected with an investigation to which a warrant issued to that agency relates.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: law enforcement access, not direct data protection

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted communication of intelligence information by ACC officers
sections.summary: Section 218 permits ACC officers to communicate, use, or record intelligence information obtained under specific legal authorities for performance of their functions. This applies only to information covered by paragraph 7A(da) of the Australian Crime Commission Act 2002. The section excludes information obtained through intercepting communications under warrants issued to other agencies or via section 66 communications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for sharing intelligence information across agencies, requiring specific legal authority and excluding certain interception methods. Relevant for DTIAs involving cross-agency data sharing.

requirements.text: ACC officers may communicate, use, or record intelligence information for performance of ACC functions covered by paragraph 7A(da) of the Australian Crime Commission Act 2002
Excludes information obtained by intercepting communications under warrants issued to other agencies
Excludes information obtained via section 66 communications where interception occurred under other agency warrants

sections.source_quote: An officer of the ACC may communicate to another person, make use of, or make a record of information mentioned in paragraph (1A)(c) for the purpose of the performance of the ACC's function covered by paragraph 7A(da) of the Australian Crime Commission Act 2002.
sections.relevance_reason: Excludes specific data types from cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted sharing of intelligence information with NACC Inspector
sections.summary: This section allows NACC Inspector and assisting personnel to share intelligence information for permitted purposes related to their functions, without sharing for any other purpose. The sharing is restricted to specific intelligence information categories and must be for authorized purposes only.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when intelligence information can be shared with NACC Inspectors, requiring specific purpose limitation and exclusivity of sharing.

requirements.text: NACC Inspector or assisting personnel may share intelligence information for permitted purposes related to their functions
Sharing must be for no other purpose than permitted purposes

sections.source_quote: The Inspector of the National Anti-Corruption Commission or a person assisting the NACC Inspector may, for a permitted purpose, or permitted purposes, in relation to the performance of a function or duty, or the exercise of a power, of the Inspector or the person assisting, and for no other purpose, communicate to another person, make use of, or make a record of the following: intelligence information;
sections.relevance_reason: Permitted sharing with specific restrictions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted communication of intercepted information to authorized recipients
sections.summary: This section specifies that lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to specific recipients based on the nature of the information, including security matters, relevant offenses, and authorized foreign jurisdictions. The communication must be for permitted purposes and follows strict recipient criteria defined in the section.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details recipient restrictions for intercepted information, including security-related communications and international transfers under Attorney-General authorization.

requirements.text: Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to Director-General of Security if related to security activities
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to Chief Executive Officer of ACC if related to 2002 matters
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to relevant authorities if related to relevant offenses
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to Inspector of Independent Commission Against Corruption (SA) if related to specific matters
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to Parliamentary Inspector of Corruption and Crime Commission (WA) if related to specific matters
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to foreign countries under Attorney-General authorization
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to International Criminal Court under Attorney-General authorization
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to War Crimes Tribunals under Attorney-General authorization
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to Australian Federal Police or ACC if related to integrity operations

sections.source_quote: if the information relates, or appears to relate, to a matter referred to in paragraph (h) of the definition of permitted purpose in subsection 5(1)—to the Parliamentary Inspector of the Corruption and Crime Commission (WA);
sections.relevance_reason: Permitted communication of intercepted data to authorized recipients

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted communication of intercepted information to specific authorities
sections.summary: This section outlines specific recipients for lawfully intercepted information, including National Anti-Corruption Commission, ASIC, and NACC Inspector, based on integrity operations, applications, and specific matters. The communication must be for permitted purposes and restricted to the defined recipients.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies recipient restrictions for intercepted information, particularly relevant for cross-agency data sharing in integrity operations.

requirements.text: Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to National Anti-Corruption Commission if related to integrity operations
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to ASIC if related to serious offenses
Lawfully intercepted information may be communicated to NACC Inspector if related to NACC corruption issues

sections.source_quote: to the Inspector of the National Anti-Corruption Commission or a person assisting the NACC Inspector, if the information relates, or appears to relate, to a NACC corruption issue (within the meaning of the National Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2022).
sections.relevance_reason: Permitted communications for lawfully intercepted data

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Restrictions on communication of intercepted information
sections.summary: This section prohibits communication of intercepted information to eligible authorities when a request under subsection (1) is in force, ensuring that information sharing is restricted to specific circumstances and authorized recipients. The communication must be for permitted purposes only.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts cross-agency communication of intercepted information during specific legal requests, relevant for DTIAs involving information sharing protocols.

requirements.text: Section 68 does not permit an officer of the other agency to communicate information to an eligible authority when a request under subsection (1) is in force

sections.source_quote: Where a request under subsection (1) is in force, section 68 does not permit an officer of the other agency to communicate information to an officer of the eligible authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Restricts sharing of intercepted data with authorities

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Permitted communication of intercepted information to specific authorities
sections.summary: This section allows individuals to communicate suspected unlawful intercepted information to specific authorities, including the Attorney-General, Minister, Director of Public Prosecutions, Commissioner of Police, and National Anti-Corruption Commissioner, for purposes related to investigations, prosecutions, or proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when intercepted information can be shared with authorities for investigations, relevant for DTIAs involving suspected unlawful communications.

requirements.text: A person may communicate suspected unlawful intercepted information to Attorney-General, Minister, Director of Public Prosecutions, Commissioner of Police, or National Anti-Corruption Commissioner
Communication must be for purposes connected with investigations, prosecutions, or proceedings

sections.source_quote: The person may communicate the relevant information to: (a) the Attorney-General; or (aa) the Minister; or (b) the Director of Public Prosecutions; or (c) the Commissioner of Police; or (ca) the National Anti-Corruption Commissioner; or (cb) the Inspector of the National Anti-Corruption Commission; or (d) the Chief Executive Officer of the ACC.
sections.relevance_reason: Permitted communication of sensitive data to authorities for investigations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Restrictions on sharing intercepted information with third parties
sections.summary: This section prohibits recipients from using, recording, or sharing lawfully intercepted information if such actions would violate another Commonwealth or State/Territory law. It applies to individuals who receive information under subsection (1) of this section and third parties who receive it from such recipients. The restrictions ensure compliance with other applicable laws when handling intercepted data.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts sharing of intercepted data across borders if it violates other laws; relevant for DTIAs involving international data transfers of intercepted communications.

requirements.text: Recipient must not communicate, use, or make a record of the information if recipient would contravene another law of the Commonwealth or a law of a State or Territory
Third person must not communicate, use, or make a record of the information if third person would contravene another law of the Commonwealth or a law of a State or Territory

sections.source_quote: If a person to whom information has been communicated in accordance with subsection 63D(2) communicates the information to another person (the recipient) under subsection (1) of this section, the recipient must not communicate, use, or make a record of, the information under subsection (1) of this section if the recipient would contravene another law of the Commonwealth, or a law of a State or Territory, in doing so.
sections.relevance_reason: Restricts sharing intercepted data with third parties

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Admissibility of intercepted information in proceedings
sections.summary: This section allows lawfully intercepted information (excluding specific types) to be used as evidence in exempt proceedings. It permits the use of interception warrant information in evidence, with the admissibility determined on a balance of probabilities for communications intercepted in contravention of subsection 7(1).
sections.dtia_summary: Allows use of intercepted data in legal proceedings; relevant for DTIAs involving international evidence sharing.

requirements.text: A person may give lawfully intercepted information (other than foreign intelligence information, general computer access intercept information, data disruption intercept information, network activity warrant intercept information or ASIO computer access intercept information) in evidence in an exempt proceeding
A person may give interception warrant information in evidence in an exempt proceeding

sections.source_quote: A person may give lawfully intercepted information (other than foreign intelligence information, general computer access intercept information, data disruption intercept information, network activity warrant intercept information or ASIO computer access intercept information) in evidence in an exempt proceeding.
sections.relevance_reason: Lawful interception and evidence admissibility

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Destruction of restricted records from pre-ordered interceptions
sections.summary: This section mandates the destruction of restricted records obtained through interceptions authorized by Part 5.3 supervisory orders that were not likely to assist in compliance with the order. It requires destruction as soon as practicable if the chief officer determines the information does not assist achieving Part 5.3 objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires deletion of intercepted data that no longer serves regulatory purposes; relevant for DTIAs involving data retention and deletion.

requirements.text: Chief officer must destroy restricted record as soon as practicable if information is not likely to assist in achieving a Part 5.3 object

sections.source_quote: If: (a) a restricted record is in the possession of an agency; (b) the restricted record relates to an interception authorised by a Part 5.3 supervisory order warrant; (c) the warrant was issued for the purpose, or for purposes that include the purpose, of obtaining information that would be likely to assist in connection with determining whether the relevant Part 5.3 supervisory order, or any succeeding Part 5.3 supervisory order, has been, or is being, complied with; (d) the interception occurred when the Part 5.3 supervisory order had been made but had not come into force; (e) the chief officer of the agency is satisfied that none of the information obtained by the interception is likely to assist in connection with achieving a Part 5.3 object; the chief officer of the agency must cause the restricted record to be destroyed as soon as practicable.
sections.relevance_reason: Destruction of restricted records under interception orders

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Destruction of restricted records from pre-ordered community safety interceptions
sections.summary: This section requires destruction of restricted records obtained through community safety supervision orders that were not likely to assist in achieving Part 9.10 objectives. It mandates destruction as soon as practicable if the chief officer determines the information does not serve the purpose of the order.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires deletion of intercepted data that no longer serves community safety purposes; relevant for DTIAs involving data retention and deletion.

requirements.text: Chief officer must destroy restricted record as soon as practicable if information is not likely to assist in achieving a Part 9.10 object

sections.source_quote: If: (a) a restricted record is in the possession of an agency; (b) the restricted record relates to an interception authorised by a community safety supervision order warrant; (c) the warrant was issued for the purpose, or for purposes that include the purpose, of obtaining information that would be likely to assist in connection with determining whether the relevant community safety supervision order, or any succeeding community safety supervision order, has been, or is being, complied with; (d) the interception occurred when the community safety supervision order had been made but had not come into force; (e) the chief officer of the agency is satisfied that none of the information obtained by the interception is likely to assist in connection with achieving a Part 9.10 object; the chief officer of the agency must cause the restricted record to be destroyed as soon as practicable.
sections.relevance_reason: Destruction of personal data under specific conditions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Record-keeping requirements for interception warrants
sections.summary: This section mandates that Commonwealth agency chief officers maintain records of warrants issued, notifications, revocations, certificates, authorizations, and communications with the Minister. It requires detailed documentation of all interception-related activities to ensure transparency and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed record-keeping of intercepted data; relevant for DTIAs involving data storage and retention.

requirements.text: Chief officer must keep each warrant issued to the agency
Chief officer must keep a copy of each notification under subsection 59A(2)
Chief officer must keep each instrument revoking a warrant
Chief officer must keep a copy of each certificate issued under subsection 61(4)
Chief officer must keep each authorisation by the chief officer under subsection 66(2)
Chief officer must keep a copy of each advice given to the Minister under subsection 103B(2)
Chief officer must keep each notice received from the Minister under paragraph 103B(3)(a) or (5)(a)

sections.source_quote: The chief officer of a Commonwealth agency must cause to be kept in the agency's records: (a) each warrant issued to the agency; and (b) a copy of each notification under subsection 59A(2), being a notification given to the Secretary of the Department; and (c) each instrument revoking such a warrant; and (d) a copy of each certificate issued under subsection 61(4) by a certifying officer of the agency; and (e) each authorisation by the chief officer under subsection 66(2); and (f) a copy of each advice the chief officer gives to the Minister under subsection 103B(2) or paragraph 103B(4)(b); and (g) each notice the chief officer receives from the Minister under paragraph 103B(3)(a) or (5)(a).
sections.relevance_reason: Record-keeping for interception warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.relevance_reason: Compilation is irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Recording reconsideration particulars for interception warrants
sections.summary: This section requires the chief officer to record in writing or electronically within practicable time any reconsideration by the chief officer under paragraph 103B(4)(a) that does not result in advice under paragraph 103B(4)(b). It also mandates that for named person warrants, the particulars must specify the service where interception occurred. Additionally, it requires the Organisation to record and provide records to the chief officer of the Commonwealth agency when the agency lacks necessary particulars.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed record-keeping for interception warrant reconsiderations, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring specific documentation of warrant-related activities and services.

requirements.text: Record reconsideration particulars in writing or via computer within practicable time after events
For named person warrants, indicate the service where interception occurred
Cause particulars to be recorded if Commonwealth agency lacks required details
Provide records to chief officer of Commonwealth agency

sections.source_quote: 238 (h) particulars of each reconsideration by the chief officer under paragraph 103B(4)(a) that does not result in the chief officer giving advice under paragraph 103B(4)(b); to be recorded in writing or by means of a computer as soon as practicable after the happening of the events to which the particulars relate or the statement relates, as the case may be.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential record-keeping for interception warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Recording warrant particulars for eligible authorities
sections.summary: This section requires eligible authorities to record and provide warrant particulars to the chief officer of the eligible authority when they lack necessary details. It specifies that the particulars must include the date of issue, issuing authority, agency, and relevant telecommunications service information for named person warrants.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures eligible authorities maintain detailed records of warrant activities, which is critical for data transfer assessments involving interception warrants and telecommunications data.

requirements.text: Cause particulars to be recorded in accordance with subsections 81(1) and (2)
Provide records to chief officer of eligible authority

sections.source_quote: 239 (c) cause those particulars to be recorded in accordance with subsections 81(1) and (2); and (d) give the records produced to the chief officer of the eligible authority to which the Part 2-5 warrant was issued.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: General Register of Warrants requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates the Secretary to maintain a General Register of Warrants containing specific details about each warrant, including the date of issue, issuing authority, agency, telecommunications service information, and the period for which the warrant is valid. It also requires regular submission of the register to the Minister for inspection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed record-keeping for interception warrants, which is essential for data transfer assessments as it specifies the data types and retention periods for warrant-related information.

requirements.text: Maintain General Register of Warrants with details including date of issue, issuing authority, agency, telecommunications service information, and validity period
Deliver General Register to Minister within 3 months after commencement of Schedule 5
Submit updated portions of General Register to Minister at least once every 3 months

sections.source_quote: 81A (1) The Secretary of the Department is to cause a General Register of Warrants to be kept. (2) The Secretary of the Department is to cause to be recorded in the General Register in relation to each Part 2-5 warrant particulars of: (a) the date of issue of the warrant; and (b) the Judge or nominated ART member who issued the warrant; and (c) the agency to which the warrant was issued; and (d) in the case of a telecommunications service warrant: (i) the telecommunications service to which the warrant relates; and (ii) the name of the person specified in the warrant as a person using or likely to use the telecommunications service; and (e) in the case of a named person warrant: (i) the name of the person to whom the warrant relates; and (ii) each telecommunications service that is specified in the warrant, or in relation to which interceptions authorised by the warrant have occurred; and (f) the period for which the warrant is to be in force; and (g) in the case of a warrant issued under subsection 46(1) or 46A(1), or issued under section 48 in the circumstances mentioned in subsection 46(1)—each serious offence in relation to which the Judge or nominated ART member who issued the warrant was satisfied, on the application for the warrant, as mentioned in:
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential record-keeping for warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Special Register of Warrants requirements
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary to maintain a Special Register of Warrants for expired warrants, including the date of issue and other relevant details. It mandates regular submission of the register to the Minister for inspection within 3 months after the commencement of Schedule 5 and subsequent updates every 3 months.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures detailed record-keeping for expired interception warrants, which is critical for data transfer assessments involving historical warrant data and compliance with retention periods.

requirements.text: Maintain Special Register of Warrants for expired warrants
Deliver Special Register to Minister within 3 months after commencement of Schedule 5
Submit updated portions of Special Register to Minister at least once every 3 months

sections.source_quote: 81D (1) Within 3 months after the commencement of Schedule 5 to the Telecommunications (Interception) Amendment Act 2006, the Secretary of the Department must deliver the Special Register to the Minister for inspection. (2) Once at least within each succeeding period of 3 months, the Secretary of the Department must deliver to the Minister, for inspection by the Minister, any part of the Special Register that represents information recorded since the Special Register, or any part of the Special Register, was last delivered to the Minister.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential record-keeping for expired warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Information provision by eligible authorities
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary to require eligible authorities to provide specific information to the Secretary for compliance with obligations under section 81C. The chief officer must provide the information within the specified period and manner.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Secretary to obtain necessary information from eligible authorities for warrant compliance assessments, which impacts data transfer assessments by ensuring timely information provision for regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Secretary may require information from chief officer of eligible authority via written notice
Chief officer must provide information within specified period and manner

sections.source_quote: 244 (2) The Secretary of the Department may, by written notice given to the chief officer of the eligible authority, require the chief officer to give the Secretary such information as the Secretary requires for the purposes of complying with the obligations imposed on him or her by section 81C. (3) The chief officer must give the information within the period, and in the manner, specified in the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman inspections of interception records
sections.summary: This section allows the Ombudsman to inspect Commonwealth agency records to assess compliance with interception warrant requirements, including specific conditions related to Part 5.3 and Part 9.10 warrants. The Ombudsman must report findings to the Minister within 3 months after each financial year end.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures oversight of interception warrant compliance through Ombudsman inspections, which is critical for data transfer assessments involving warrant-related data and compliance with retention periods.

requirements.text: Ombudsman may inspect agency records for compliance with interception warrant requirements
Ombudsman must report inspection results to Minister within 3 months after financial year end

sections.source_quote: 84 (1) The Ombudsman shall, as soon as practicable, and in any event within 3 months, after the end of each financial year, report to the Minister in writing, in relation to each Commonwealth agency, about the results of the inspections under subsections 83(1), (3) and (4), during that financial year, of the agency's records.
sections.relevance_reason: Ombudsman inspections for compliance with interception warrants

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman reporting on agency contraventions and inspections
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may report to the Minister in writing about inspection results at any time, including when requested. If an inspection reveals an agency officer has contravened an Act provision, the Ombudsman may include a contravention report. This section excludes contraventions of specific sections (79, 79AA, 79AB, 80, 81) from this reporting requirement. The Ombudsman must comply with disclosure obligations for intercepted information when reporting.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes when the Ombudsman must report agency contraventions to the Minister, relevant for DTIAs involving agency compliance with data protection laws.

requirements.text: The Ombudsman may report to the Minister in writing at any time about the results of an inspection under this Part
The Ombudsman shall give a copy of a report under subsection (1) or (2) to the chief officer of the agency to which the report relates
If the Ombudsman is of the opinion that an agency officer has contravened an Act provision, the Ombudsman may include a contravention report in the inspection report
A contravention of a condition or restriction in a warrant is a contravention of an Act provision
Subsection (1) does not apply to contraventions of sections 79, 79AA, 79AB, 80, or 81

sections.source_quote: The Ombudsman may report to the Minister in writing at any time about the results of an inspection under this Part and shall do so if so requested by the Minister.
sections.relevance_reason: Ombudsman reporting on agency contraventions

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's annual report on agency contraventions
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may include reports on contraventions in annual reports if notified under specific subsections but did not conduct inspections during the relevant period. This section clarifies that notification timing (before, during, or after the financial year) does not affect the report inclusion, and does not limit other report content.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies when the Ombudsman can include contravention reports in annual reports, relevant for DTIAs involving agency compliance reporting.

requirements.text: In a report under subsection 84(1) for a financial year, the Ombudsman may include a contravention report if notified under subsection 59B(2) and did not conduct inspections
Notification under subsection 59B(2) may occur before, during, or after the financial year
Subsection (1) does not limit what the Ombudsman may include in reports under sections 84 or 85

sections.source_quote: In a report under subsection 84(1) in relation to a financial year, the Ombudsman may include a report on a contravention of which the Ombudsman is notified under subsection 59B(2) (about Part 5.3 warrants), if the Ombudsman does not conduct an inspection under subsection 83(3) in relation to a period during which the contravention occurred.
sections.relevance_reason: Ombudsman's report on contraventions not directly related to data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's access to agency records and premises
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may enter agency premises, access all records, and make copies of records without restriction. The chief officer must assist the Ombudsman with information relevant to inspections. These powers are not subject to other laws, and include incidental actions necessary for inspections.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the Ombudsman broad access to agency records and premises, relevant for DTIAs involving data access and inspection powers.

requirements.text: The Ombudsman may enter agency premises after notifying the chief officer
The Ombudsman is entitled to full and free access to all agency records at reasonable times
The Ombudsman is entitled to full and free access to the General Register and Special Register
The Ombudsman may make copies of and take extracts from agency records
The Ombudsman may require agency officers to provide relevant information
The chief officer must ensure agency officers provide assistance to the Ombudsman
The Ombudsman's powers include incidental actions necessary for inspections

sections.source_quote: The Ombudsman may, after notifying the chief officer of the agency, enter at any reasonable time premises occupied by the agency
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general governance for inspections

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's requirements for officer attendance and information
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may require officers to attend for questioning, provide information, or answer questions relevant to inspections. Officers cannot refuse to comply with these requirements. The specified time, place, and period must be reasonable, and the Ombudsman may require the chief officer to attend if the officer's identity is unknown.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the Ombudsman's authority to require officer attendance and information, relevant for DTIAs involving data access and inspection procedures.

requirements.text: The Ombudsman may require an officer to attend before a specified inspecting officer
The Ombudsman may require an officer to attend at a specified place within a specified period
The Ombudsman may require the chief officer to attend if the officer's identity is unknown
The specified time, place, and period must be reasonable
An officer cannot refuse to attend, provide information, or answer questions

sections.source_quote: The Ombudsman may, by writing given to the officer, require the officer to attend: (a) before a specified inspecting officer; (b) at a specified place; and (c) within a specified period or at a specified time on a specified day
sections.relevance_reason: Ombudsman's procedural requirements for officers

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's immunity from legal liability for information
sections.summary: The Ombudsman cannot be sued for actions taken in good faith during inspections, as long as the information, answers, or documents are not admissible in evidence against the person except in prosecutions for offenses under section 107. This section also ensures officers can provide information or access to records without legal barriers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides immunity for the Ombudsman's actions during inspections, relevant for DTIAs involving data access and reporting.

requirements.text: A person is not excused from giving information, answering questions, or providing access to documents when required by this Part
Information, answers, or documents obtained under this Part are not admissible in evidence against the person except in prosecutions for offenses under section 107
Officers may give information to inspecting officers without legal barriers
Officers may provide access to agency records without legal barriers

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding any other law, a person is not excused from giving information, answering a question, or giving access to a document, as and when required by or under this Part, on the ground that giving the information, answering the question, or giving access to the document, as the case may be, would contravene a law
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: legal immunity for Ombudsman

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's sharing of information for inspections
sections.summary: Information provided to inspecting officers for inspections or reports may be shared with other inspecting officers, used, or recorded for the purpose of inspections or reports. This section ensures that information obtained under this Part is not subject to other laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows sharing of information for inspections, relevant for DTIAs involving data access and reporting procedures.

requirements.text: Inspecting officers may communicate information to other inspecting officers for inspections or reports
Inspecting officers may make use of information obtained during inspections
Inspecting officers may make records of information for inspections or reports

sections.source_quote: Where information is given or communicated to an inspecting officer, as permitted by subsection 88(2) or this section, for the purposes of an inspection, or of a report on an inspection, under this Part of an agency's records; the inspecting officer may, notwithstanding section 63 or any other law, communicate to another inspecting officer, make use of, or make a record of, the information for the purposes of an inspection, or of a report on an inspection
sections.relevance_reason: Inspecting officers' use of personal info for compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: State agency definitions and information exchange with inspecting authorities
sections.summary: This section defines 'State agency' as an eligible authority of a State that is an agency, and 'State inspecting authority' as the authority that inspects State agencies under relevant State law. It establishes that the Ombudsman may share information related to State agencies obtained under this Act with the State inspecting authority, but only if the Ombudsman is satisfied the sharing is necessary for the authority's functions. The Ombudsman may also receive information from State inspecting authorities relevant to their functions.

requirements.text: State agency means an eligible authority of a State that is an agency

State inspecting authority, in relation to a State agency, means the authority that, under the law of the State concerned, has the function of making inspections of the kind referred to in paragraph 35(1)(h) of this Act, or section 9A of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986, when the State agency is exercising powers under the law of that State that is of a similar nature to this Act
The Ombudsman may give information that relates to a State agency and was obtained by the Ombudsman under this Act to the authority that is the State inspecting authority in relation to the agency

The Ombudsman may only give information to an authority under subsection (2) if the Ombudsman is satisfied that the giving of the information is necessary to enable the authority to perform its functions in relation to the State agency
The Ombudsman may receive from a State inspecting authority information relevant to the performance of the Ombudsman's functions under this Act

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Information exchange between IGIS and State inspecting authorities
sections.summary: This section defines the conditions under which the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security (IGIS) may share information related to State agencies with State inspecting authorities. The IGIS may provide information that relates to State agencies, was obtained under the IGIS Act 1986, and relates to the use of powers under this Act by an intelligence agency, with the State inspecting authority. The sharing is permitted only if the IGIS is satisfied it is necessary for the authority to perform its functions regarding the State agency.

requirements.text: The Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security may give information that relates to a State agency and was obtained by an IGIS official under the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986

The Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security may receive from a State inspecting authority information that relates to the use of powers under this Act by an intelligence agency and is relevant to the performance of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security's functions under that Act

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Annual reports by State authorities on information derived from records
sections.summary: This section requires State authorities to report to the Minister whether information that section 102 or 102A requires in the Minister's report can be derived from the authority's records. If such information can be derived, the report must set out that information; otherwise, it must state that no such information can be derived. The report must also include a statement of total expenditure incurred by the authority in executing warrants during the year.

requirements.text: A report under subsection (3) must include a statement of the total expenditure (including expenditure of a capital nature) incurred by the agency concerned in connection with the execution of warrants during the year to which the report relates

sections.source_quote: 256 Compilation (3A) A report under subsection (3) must include a statement of the total expenditure (including expenditure of a capital nature) incurred by the agency concerned in connection with the execution of warrants during the year to which the report relates.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Annual reports by State authorities on emergency interception actions
sections.summary: This section requires State authorities to report to the Minister on emergency interception actions taken without warrants or after warrant applications were refused. The report must detail the date, time, duration of interceptions, reasons for not intercepting communications under a telecommunications service warrant, information about the use of intercepted information, communication of information to third parties, and the number of arrests made based on intercepted information.

requirements.text: The report must contain the following information: (a) if an interception occurred: (i) the date and time at which the interception began; (ii) the duration of the interception
The report must contain the following information: (b) if there was no interception but action had been taken to cause a communication to be intercepted—details of the action taken
The report must contain the following information: (c) the reasons it would not have been effective to intercept the communications under a telecommunications service warrant
The report must contain the following information: (d) information about the use made by the agency of information obtained by each interception
The report must contain the following information: (e) information about the communication of such information to persons other than officers of the agency
The report must contain the following information: (f) the number of arrests that have been, or are likely to be, made on the basis of such information
The report must contain the following information: (g) an assessment of the usefulness of information obtained by each interception

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Annual reports by State authorities on information derived from records
sections.summary: This section requires State authorities to report to the Minister whether information that section 102 or 102A requires in the Minister's report can be derived from the authority's records. If such information can be derived, the report must set out that information; otherwise, it must state that no such information can be derived. The report must also include a statement of total expenditure incurred by the authority in executing warrants during the year.

requirements.text: The chief officer of an eligible authority of a State shall, as soon as practicable, and in any event within 3 months, after each 30 June, give to the Minister a written report that: (a) if information that section 102 or 102A requires to be set out in the Minister's report under Division 2 relating to the year ending on that 30 June can be derived from the authority's records—sets out that information; or (b) in any other case—states that no such information can be derived

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Annual report by Minister on warrants under Part 2-5
sections.summary: This section requires the Minister to prepare an annual report that relates to the year ending on 30 June and complies with the Division. The report must include statistics on applications for Part 2-5 warrants, telephone applications, renewal applications, applications with requests for entry on premises, and conditions or restrictions related to interceptions under warrants.

requirements.text: The Minister shall, as soon as practicable after each 30 June, cause to be prepared a written report that relates to the year ending on that 30 June and complies with this Division

sections.source_quote: 260 Compilation The Minister shall, as soon as practicable after each 30 June, cause to be prepared a written report that relates to the year ending on that 30 June and complies with this Division.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting requirements for warrant statistics
sections.summary: This section mandates that reports must include specific statistics about telecommunications devices, intercepted communications, and serious offences specified in warrants. The report must detail the number of telecommunications services intercepted, the total number of devices, and categories of serious offences for each Commonwealth agency and eligible State authority. The requirements apply to agencies and authorities that were active during the reporting year.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies statistical reporting obligations for warrant activities, which could impact data transfer assessments by requiring detailed tracking of intercepted communications and their legal use.

requirements.text: Report must include total number of telecommunications services intercepted under warrants
Report must include total number of telecommunications devices by which communications were intercepted
Report must include categories of serious offences specified in warrants
Report must include how many serious offences in each category were specified

sections.source_quote: 263 Compilation ... the total number of telecommunications services intercepted under those of the warrants that did authorise the interception of communications made by means of a telecommunications device or telecommunications devices identified in the warrant; and (iii) the total number of telecommunications devices by which communications were intercepted under those of the warrants that did authorise the interception of communications made by means of a telecommunications device or telecommunications devices identified in the warrant; and
sections.relevance_reason: Pure reporting requirement for intercepted communications

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Warrant renewal duration definitions
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes a 90-day, 150-day, or 180-day final renewal warrant. It specifies that such renewals must be the last renewal of an original warrant that ceased to be in force within specific timeframes after the original warrant was issued. The definition applies to the reporting requirements for warrant effectiveness.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies the duration definitions for warrant renewals, which may affect how long data can be retained under warrant authority.

requirements.text: A 90-day final renewal refers to a warrant that ceased to be in force more than 90 days but not more than 150 days after original warrant issuance
A 150-day final renewal refers to a warrant that ceased to be in force more than 150 days but not more than 180 days after original warrant issuance
An 180-day final renewal refers to a warrant that ceased to be in force more than 180 days after original warrant issuance

sections.source_quote: 265 Compilation ... A reference in subsection (1) or (2) to a 90 day final renewal, to a 150 day final renewal or to a 180 day final renewal is a reference to a warrant: (a) that is the last renewal of an original warrant; and (b) that ceased to be in force: (i) more than 90 days but not more than 150 days; (ii) more than 150 days but not more than 180 days; or (iii) more than 180 days; as the case may be, after the day of issue of that original warrant.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines warrant renewal timeframes for compliance reporting

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Warrant effectiveness reporting metrics
sections.summary: This section requires reports to include metrics on the effectiveness of warrants, such as the number of arrests made and prosecutions that ended using lawfully intercepted information. It specifies that the report must detail how many arrests were made in connection with agency functions or based on intercepted information, and the number of prosecutions for specific offence categories with convictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines how warrant effectiveness is measured, which could impact data transfer assessments by requiring tracking of intercepted data's legal use in prosecutions.

requirements.text: Report must include number of arrests made during the year in connection with agency functions
Report must include number of arrests made during the year based on lawfully intercepted information
Report must include categories of prescribed offences for which prosecutions ended using intercepted information
Report must include number of offences in each category with convictions

sections.source_quote: 266 Compilation ... the report shall set out, for each Commonwealth agency, for each eligible Commonwealth authority, and for each eligible authority of a State: (a) how many arrests were made during that year: (i) in connection with the performance by the agency or authority of its functions; and (ii) on the basis of information that was or included lawfully intercepted information; (b) the categories of the prescribed offences proceedings by way of prosecutions for which ended during that year, being proceedings in which, according to the records of the agency or authority, lawfully intercepted information was given in evidence; and (c) in relation to each of those categories: (i) the number of such offences in that category; and (ii) the number of such offences in that category in respect of which convictions were recorded.
sections.relevance_reason: Warrant reporting metrics for law enforcement

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Warrant eligibility calculation
sections.summary: This section defines the calculation for eligible warrants in the report, specifying that eligible warrants are those that were issued to an agency or authority, were in force during the reporting year, and for which a prosecution was instituted or likely to be instituted based on intercepted information. It clarifies how warrants are categorized for effectiveness metrics.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides the formula for determining eligible warrants, which could impact data transfer assessments by defining when intercepted data is legally actionable.

requirements.text: Eligible warrants are those that were issued to the agency or authority
Eligible warrants were in force during the reporting year
Eligible warrants had prosecutions instituted or likely to be instituted based on intercepted information

sections.source_quote: 267 Compilation ... Eligible warrants means the number of warrants that satisfy the following conditions: (a) the warrant was issued to the agency or authority, as the case requires; (b) the warrant was in force during the year to which the report relates; (c) a prosecution was instituted, or was likely to be instituted, on the basis of information obtained by interceptions under: (i) if the warrant was a renewal of an original warrant: (A) the original warrant; or (B) any other renewal of the original warrant; or (ii) if the warrant was an original warrant—any renewal of the original warrant.
sections.relevance_reason: Warrant eligibility calculation for reporting

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Warrant expenditure reporting
sections.summary: This section requires reports to include the total expenditure incurred by agencies or authorities in executing warrants during the reporting year, the number of warrants, and information about judge availability and interceptions carried out on behalf of other agencies or authorities. It specifies the formula for calculating warrant expenditure per warrant.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details financial reporting obligations for warrant activities, which could impact data transfer assessments by requiring tracking of costs associated with intercepted data use.

requirements.text: Report must include total expenditure incurred by agency or authority in executing warrants during the year
Report must include number of warrants to which total expenditure relates
Report must include information about judge availability to issue warrants
Report must include number of interceptions carried out on behalf of other agencies or authorities

Number of warrants means the number of warrants to which the total warrant expenditure relates; and information about the availability of judges to issue warrants under Part 2-5 and the extent to which nominated ART members have been used for that purpose, but not including information that would identify a particular judge or ART member; and the number (if any) of interceptions carried out on behalf of each other such Commonwealth agency or eligible authority
sections.relevance_reason: Pure expenditure reporting, no data protection link

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Deferral of information in reports
sections.summary: This section allows for the deferral of certain information in reports if the chief officer determines that the information constitutes Part 5.3 or Part 9.10 information. It requires the chief officer to advise the Minister in writing not to include such information in the next Ministerial report.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a mechanism to exclude sensitive information from reports, which could impact data transfer assessments by allowing deferral of specific data categories.

sections.title: Ministerial report exclusion for sensitive information
sections.summary: The Minister must notify the chief officer in writing if the Minister is satisfied that information is Part 5.3 or Part 9.10 information. The Minister must not include such information in any Ministerial report until the Minister decides otherwise under subsection (5). If information is excluded, the chief officer must reconsider whether it qualifies as sensitive information before the next report and advise the Minister to include it if it no longer qualifies.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Preservation of stored communications for law enforcement
sections.summary: This section outlines a system for preserving stored communications held by carriers to prevent destruction before access under warrants. Carriers must preserve communications related to specified persons or services when given preservation notices by agencies. There are two types of preservation notices: domestic (for Australian law or security matters) and foreign (for foreign law or international offenses).

requirements.text: Carriers must preserve stored communications held that relate to specified person or telecommunications service
Preservation notices can be domestic (for Australian law or security) or foreign (for foreign law or international offenses)

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Access to stored communications for law enforcement
sections.summary: This section specifies when access to stored communications is permitted by law enforcement agencies. Access is allowed for specific purposes including investigating listening devices, determining device locations, and enforcement activities under the Spam Act 2003. The access must be under warrants or authorizations that meet specific legal criteria.

requirements.text: Access to stored communications for discovering listening devices at a place
Access to stored communications for determining location of listening device
Access to stored communications by ASIO employees for lawful duties under the Act

sections.summary: This section lists specific agencies authorized to access stored communications under certain conditions. sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Declaration of criminal law-enforcement agencies
sections.summary: This section details the process for declaring an authority or body as a criminal law-enforcement agency. The Minister may declare an authority or body as such if it meets criteria including investigating serious contraventions, compliance with privacy principles, and having processes to ensure compliance with obligations. The declaration may be subject to conditions and can be revoked if circumstances change.

requirements.text: Minister may declare authority or body as criminal law-enforcement agency
Minister must be satisfied that authority or body investigates serious contraventions
Authority or body must comply with Australian Privacy Principles or binding schemes
