sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Privacy protection requirements for law enforcement agencies
sections.summary: This section specifies that any binding scheme providing protection for personal information must be comparable to the Australian Privacy Principles, include monitoring mechanisms for compliance, and allow individuals to seek recourse if their information is mishandled. The scheme must ensure that the authority or body complies with privacy obligations when accessing personal information.

requirements.text: Privacy scheme must be comparable to Australian Privacy Principles
Privacy scheme must include monitoring mechanism for compliance
Privacy scheme must include recourse mechanism for mishandled information

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Authorisation for legislative declarations

Declarations under subsection (3) come into force when made or on a later specified date

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Conditions for issuing stored communications warrants
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which an issuing authority may issue a stored communications warrant. The authority must be satisfied that Division 1 has been complied with, the person has made or another person has made communications for which they are the intended recipient, and that accessing the communications would likely assist in investigating a serious contravention or foreign contravention. The issuing authority must also consider the gravity of the conduct, potential interference with the investigation, and whether alternative investigative methods have been used.

requirements.text: The issuing authority must be satisfied that Division 1 has been complied with
The person must have made communications or another person must have made communications for which they are the intended recipient
Accessing stored communications would likely assist in investigating a serious contravention or foreign contravention

The issuing authority must consider how much information would assist in the investigation
The issuing authority must consider whether alternative investigative methods have been used

sections.heading: Compilation

For international assistance applications, similar considerations apply but with specific focus on foreign contraventions.

requirements.text: The issuing authority must consider potential interference with the investigation by accessing stored communications

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Scope and form of stored communications warrants
sections.summary: This section defines what a stored communications warrant authorises and specifies the form and content requirements for such warrants. The warrant authorises access to communications made by the person or another person for whom they are the intended recipient, and must be in accordance with prescribed form and signed by the issuing authority. The warrant becomes effective when issued and must be executed before the communication is accessed.

requirements.text: A stored communications warrant authorises access to communications made by the person or another person for whom they are the intended recipient

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: This section specifies the conditions under which stored communications warrants come into force and the limitations on their authority. sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Technical assistance for warrant execution
sections.summary: This section permits designated officers or employees of carriers to provide technical assistance to criminal law-enforcement agency personnel exercising authority under a stored communications warrant. requirements.text: Designated officers or employees of carriers may provide technical assistance to criminal law-enforcement agency personnel

sections.heading: Compilation

sections.summary: This section permits the Director-General of Security to share foreign intelligence information with ASIO employees or affiliates during the performance of the Organisation's functions. sections.heading: Compilation

requirements.text: The Director-General of Security may communicate foreign intelligence information to another person for purposes approved by the Attorney-General in writing

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notification requirements for authorisations and revocations
sections.summary: This section requires that ASIO employees or affiliates must notify the person from whom disclosure is sought when an authorisation is made under Division 3 of Part 4-1. It also mandates that relevant staff members of enforcement agencies must notify the person when an authorisation is revoked under specific subsections.

requirements.text: If a person makes an authorisation under Division 3 of Part 4-1, an ASIO employee or ASIO affiliate must notify the person from whom the disclosure is sought
If a person revokes an authorisation under subsection 176(6), an ASIO employee or ASIO affiliate must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation

If an authorised officer of an enforcement agency makes an authorisation under Division 4 of Part 4-1, a relevant staff member of the enforcement agency must notify the person from whom the disclosure is sought

If an authorised officer of a criminal law-enforcement agency revokes an authorisation under subsection 180(7), a relevant staff member of the enforcement agency must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation

If an authorised officer of the Australian Federal Police makes an authorisation under subsection 180A(2) or 180B(2), or extends the period for which an authorisation is in force under subsection 180B(6), a relevant staff member of the Australian Federal Police must notify the person from whom the disclosure is sought

If an authorised officer of the Australian Federal Police revokes an authorisation under subsection 180B(4), a relevant staff member of the Australian Federal Police must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation

sections.source_quote: 391 (2) If, under subsection 176(6), a person revokes an authorisation, an ASIO employee or ASIO affiliate must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation. (3) If an authorised officer of an enforcement agency makes an authorisation under Division 4 of Part 4-1, a relevant staff member of the enforcement agency must notify the person from whom the disclosure is sought. (4) If, under subsection 180(7), an authorised officer of a criminal law-enforcement agency revokes an authorisation, a relevant staff member of the enforcement agency must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation. (5) If an authorised officer of the Australian Federal Police makes an authorisation under subsection 180A(2) or 180B(2), or extends the period for which an authorisation is in force under subsection 180B(6), a relevant staff member of the Australian Federal Police must notify the person from whom the disclosure is sought. (6) If, under subsection 180B(4), an authorised officer of the Australian Federal Police revokes an authorisation, a relevant staff member of the Australian Federal Police must notify the person who was notified of the authorisation.
sections.relevance_reason: Notification requirements for authorisations/revocations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Evidentiary certificates for acts by ASIO
sections.summary: This section allows the Director-General of Security or Deputy Director-General of Security to issue written certificates signed by them, detailing relevant facts about actions taken by ASIO employees or affiliates in connection with disclosures covered by authorisations under Division 3 or 4 of Part 4-1.

requirements.text: The Director-General of Security or Deputy Director-General of Security may issue a written certificate signed by them setting out relevant facts about actions taken by ASIO employees or affiliates in connection with disclosures covered by authorisations under Division 3 or 4 of Part 4-1
The certificate must be signed by the Director-General of Security or Deputy Director-General of Security
The certificate must set out relevant facts about actions taken by ASIO employees or affiliates in connection with disclosures covered by authorisations under Division 3 or 4 of Part 4-1

sections.source_quote: 393 (1) The Director-General of Security or the Deputy Director-General of Security may issue a written certificate signed by him or her setting out such facts as he or she considers relevant with respect to: (a) anything done by an ASIO employee or ASIO affiliate in connection with the disclosure of information or a document covered by an authorisation in force under a provision of Division 3 or 4 of Part 4-1; or (b) anything done by an ASIO employee or ASIO affiliate in connection with the disclosure of information or a document, covered by such an authorisation; or

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Notification requirements for authorisations intended to identify media sources
sections.summary: This section requires that when a journalist information warrant is issued under Subdivision B of Division 4C of Part 4-1, the Director-General of Security must notify the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security, and the Attorney-General must notify the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security.

requirements.text: The Director-General of Security must give a copy of the warrant to the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security as soon as practicable
The Attorney-General must cause the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security to be notified of the issuing of the warrant as soon as practicable

sections.source_quote: 394 (1) If a journalist information warrant is issued under Subdivision B of Division 4C of Part 4-1: (a) the Director-General of Security must, as soon as practicable, give a copy of the warrant to the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security; and (b) the Attorney-General must, as soon as practicable, cause the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security to be notified of the issuing of the warrant.

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting requirements for journalist information warrants
sections.summary: This section mandates that if the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security provides a report to the Minister under specific sections of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986, and certain authorisations are issued, the Minister must notify the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security. It also requires that enforcement agencies provide copies of journalist information warrants to relevant authorities promptly after issuance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting of journalist information warrants to oversight bodies, relevant for DTIAs involving intelligence data transfers to foreign jurisdictions.
sections.authority: Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security

requirements.text: If Inspector-General gives report to Minister under section 22 or 25A of Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986, Minister must notify Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security
If journalist information warrant issued to enforcement agency, chief officer must give copies to Minister and Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security

sections.source_quote: the Inspector-General gives to the Minister a report under section 22 or 25A of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986; and following: (i) a journalist information warrant issued to the Organisation; (ii) one or more authorisations referred to in subsection (2) of this section

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting requirements for Australian Federal Police warrants
sections.summary: This section specifies that when the Australian Federal Police receives a journalist information warrant, the Commissioner must provide copies to the Minister and the Ombudsman. The Minister must then notify the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security. It also requires that authorisations made under such warrants be reported to the Ombudsman.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting of journalist information warrants to oversight bodies, relevant for DTIAs involving intelligence data transfers to foreign jurisdictions.

requirements.text: If Australian Federal Police receives journalist information warrant, Commissioner must give copies to Minister and Ombudsman
Minister must notify Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security after receiving warrant copy
If authorisation made under warrant, chief officer must give copy to Ombudsman

sections.source_quote: the Commissioner of Police must, as soon as practicable, give copies of the warrant to the Minister and the Ombudsman

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman briefing requests for journalist warrants
sections.summary: This section allows the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security to request briefings from the Ombudsman regarding journalist information warrants or authorisations that relate to specific reports made to the Minister. It applies when the Ombudsman provides a report under section 186J of the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides oversight mechanism for intelligence data transfers through journalist warrants, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border data flows.
sections.authority: Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security

requirements.text: Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security may request briefing from Ombudsman on journalist information warrant or authorisations

sections.source_quote: The Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security may request a briefing from the Ombudsman on: (a) a journalist information warrant; or (b) an authorisation or authorisations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Reporting requirements for retained data access
sections.summary: This section requires that if the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security provides a report to the Minister under specific sections of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986, and the report relates to access to retained data through authorisations, the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security may request briefings from the Inspector-General on those authorisations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires oversight of retained data access mechanisms, relevant for DTIAs involving intelligence data transfers.
sections.authority: Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security

requirements.text: If Inspector-General gives report to Minister under section 22 or 25A of Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security Act 1986, and report relates to access to retained data by authorisations, Parliamentary Joint Committee may request briefing from Inspector-General

sections.source_quote: The Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security may request a briefing from the Inspector-General on the authorisation or authorisations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Annual reporting on authorisations by enforcement agencies
sections.summary: This section requires enforcement agencies to submit annual reports to the Minister detailing the number of authorisations made under various sections of the Act, including those related to criminal law enforcement, journalist information warrants, and disclosures to foreign countries. The reports must include specific categories of offences and information held.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates detailed reporting on intelligence authorisations, critical for assessing cross-border data flows and compliance with international data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: Enforcement agency must submit annual report to Minister by 3 months after each 30 June
Report must include number of authorisations made under sections 178, 178A, 179, 180, 180A, 180B, 180C, 180D
Report must include number of disclosures to foreign countries by Australian Federal Police
Report must include offences and other matters for which authorisations were made
Report must specify the lengths of time information or documents were held

sections.source_quote: As soon as practicable, and in any event within 3 months, after each 30 June, the head (however described) of an enforcement agency must give the Minister a written report
sections.relevance_reason: Enforcement agency reporting on authorisations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Detailed reporting on retained data authorisations
sections.summary: This section requires enforcement agencies to provide detailed annual reports to the Minister on the number of authorisations made for retained data, including specific categories of information held, the duration of data retention, and disclosures to foreign countries. The reports must categorize authorizations by time periods and exclude certain authorizations from specific calculations.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides granular reporting on intelligence data transfers, essential for DTIAs assessing cross-border data flows and compliance with international data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: Report must include number of authorisations for retained data with specific information types
Report must specify the lengths of time information or documents were held in 8 successive 3-month periods
Report must exclude authorizations under subsection 180(2) except where they include authorizations under subsection 180(3)

sections.source_quote: the head (however described) of an enforcement agency must give the Minister a written report that relates to the year ending on that 30 June
sections.relevance_reason: Detailed reporting on retained data authorisations

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Minister's report compilation requirements
sections.summary: The Minister must prepare a report containing specified information for each report under subsection (1), excluding certain information. The report may include additional information the Minister deems appropriate. The Minister must lay a copy of the report before each House of Parliament within 15 sitting days after completion. The report must not enable identification of a person.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires the Minister to compile and publish reports that must not identify individuals, relevant for data transfer impact assessments involving government records.

requirements.text: The Minister must prepare a report containing the information set out in each report under subsection (1), other than the information referred to in paragraph (1)(cb).
The report may contain any other information the Minister considers appropriate.
The Minister must cause a copy of a report under subsection (2) to be laid before each House of the Parliament within 15 sitting days of that House after the day on which the report was completed.
A report under this section must not be made in a manner that is likely to enable the identification of a person.

sections.source_quote: The Minister must prepare a report that contains the information set out in each report under subsection (1), other than the information referred to in paragraph (1)(cb). The report may contain any other information the Minister considers appropriate. The Minister must cause a copy of a report under subsection (2) to be laid before each House of the Parliament within 15 sitting days of that House after the day on which the report was completed. A report under this section must not be made in a manner that is likely to enable the identification of a person.
sections.relevance_reason: General governance, not specific data compliance

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Enforcement agency record retention requirements
sections.summary: The chief officer of an enforcement agency must keep specific records for a period of 3 years from their creation. These records include authorisations, revocations, disclosures, uses of information, evidentiary certificates, reports to the Minister, and other prescribed documents. The Minister may prescribe additional documents to be retained.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed record retention for enforcement agencies, critical for assessing data transfer impacts involving government records and third-party data.

requirements.text: The chief officer of an enforcement agency must cause the following, or copies of the following, to be kept in the agency's records for the period specified in subsection (3):
Each authorisation made by an authorised officer of the agency under section 178, 178A, 179 or 180, and documents or other materials that indicate whether the authorisation was properly made.
Each notice of the revocation under subsection 180(7) of an authorisation under section 180, and documents or other materials that indicate whether the revocation was properly made.
Each evidentiary certificate issued under section 185C.
Each report given to the Minister under section 186.
Documents and other materials of a kind prescribed under subsection (2) of this section.

The period for which the chief officer of an enforcement agency must cause a particular item to be kept in the agency's records under subsection (1) of this section is the period starting when the item came into existence and ending after 3 years.
The Minister may, by legislative instrument, prescribe kinds of documents and other materials that the chief officer of an enforcement agency must cause to be kept in the agency's records.

sections.source_quote: The chief officer of an enforcement agency must cause the following, or copies of the following, to be kept in the agency's records for the period specified in subsection (3): (a) each authorisation made by an authorised officer of the agency under section 178, 178A, 179 or 180, and documents or other materials that indicate any of the following: (i) whether the authorisation was properly made (including whether the authorised officer took into account the matters referred to in subsection 178(3), 178A(3), 179(3) or 180(4) (as the case requires), the matters referred to in section 180F and all other relevant considerations); (ii) if the authorisation is made under section 180—the period during which the authorisation is in force; (iii) when the authorisation was notified under subsection 184(3); (b) each notice of the revocation under subsection 180(7) of an authorisation under section 180, and documents or other materials that indicate any of the following: (i) whether the revocation was properly made; (c) each evidentiary certificate issued under section 185C; (d) each report given to the Minister under section 186; (e) documents and other materials of a kind prescribed under subsection (2) of this section.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for enforcement agency

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's record inspection powers
sections.summary: The Ombudsman must inspect records of enforcement agencies to determine compliance with Chapter 4 and, if applicable, Chapter 3. The Ombudsman may inspect records related to stored communications warrants or authorisations under specific telecommunications laws. The Ombudsman must give reasonable notice before inspections and may enter premises and access records.
sections.dtia_summary: This section grants the Ombudsman authority to inspect records for compliance, relevant for assessing data transfer impacts involving government records and third-party data.

requirements.text: The Ombudsman must inspect records of an enforcement agency, except the ACC or any agency, or part of an agency performing any functions, prescribed by regulations made under the Ombudsman Act 1976 for the purposes of subsection (1B) of this section, to determine the extent of compliance with Chapter 4 by the agency and its officers.

If the agency is a criminal law-enforcement agency, except the ACC or any agency, or part of an agency performing any functions, prescribed by regulations made under the Ombudsman Act 1976 for the purposes of subsection (1B) of this section, the Ombudsman must inspect records to determine the extent of compliance with Chapter 3 by the agency and its officers.
The Ombudsman may inspect records related to stored communications warrants or authorisations under Division 3, 4 or 4A of Part 4-1 of the Telecommunications Act 1997.
The Ombudsman must give reasonable notice to the chief officer of the agency of when the inspection will occur.
The Ombudsman may enter at any reasonable time premises occupied by the agency.
The Ombudsman is entitled to have full and free access at all reasonable times to all records of the agency that are relevant to the inspection.
The Ombudsman may make copies of, and take extracts from, records of the agency.
The Ombudsman may require a member of staff of the agency to give the Ombudsman any information that the Ombudsman considers necessary.

sections.source_quote: The Ombudsman must inspect records of an enforcement agency, except the ACC or any agency, or part of an agency performing any functions, prescribed by regulations made under the Ombudsman Act 1976 for the purposes of subsection (1B) of this section, to determine: (a) the extent of compliance with Chapter 4 by the agency and its officers; and (b) if the agency is a criminal law-enforcement agency, except the ACC or any agency, or part of an agency performing any functions, prescribed by regulations made under the Ombudsman Act 1976 for the purposes of subsection (1B) of this section—the extent of compliance with Chapter 3 by the agency and its officers.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for record inspection

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman's power to obtain information
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may require officers to provide information or attend to answer questions relevant to inspections. The Ombudsman must specify a reasonable place and time for attendance. Officers cannot refuse to provide information, answer questions, or attend.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables the Ombudsman to obtain information from enforcement agencies, relevant for assessing data transfer impacts involving government records and third-party data.

requirements.text: If the Ombudsman has reasonable grounds to believe that an officer of a particular enforcement agency is able to give information relevant to an inspection under this Chapter of the agency's records, the Ombudsman may require the officer to do one or both of the following: (i) give the information to the Ombudsman, by writing signed by the officer, at a specified place and within a specified period; (ii) attend before a specified inspecting officer to answer questions relevant to the inspection.
A requirement under subsection (1) to attend before an inspecting officer must specify a place for the attendance and a period within which, or a time and day when, the attendance is to occur.
A person must not refuse to attend before a person, to give information, or to answer questions when required to do so under this section.

sections.source_quote: If the Ombudsman has reasonable grounds to believe that an officer of a particular enforcement agency is able to give information relevant to an inspection under this Chapter of the agency's records, the Ombudsman may: (a) if the Ombudsman knows the officer's identity—by writing given to the officer, require the officer to do one or both of the following: (i) give the information to the Ombudsman, by writing signed by the officer, at a specified place and within a specified period; (ii) attend before a specified inspecting officer to answer questions relevant to the inspection.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: Ombudsman's procedural power for inspections

sections.heading: Compilation
sections.title: Ombudsman delegation powers
sections.summary: The Ombudsman may delegate powers to APS employees or persons with similar oversight functions. Delegates must provide the instrument of delegation upon request by affected persons.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the Ombudsman to delegate powers, relevant for assessing data transfer impacts involving government records and third-party data.

requirements.text: The Ombudsman may delegate to an APS employee responsible to the Ombudsman or to a person having similar oversight functions to the Ombudsman under the law of a State or Territory or to an employee responsible to that person all or any of the Ombudsman's powers under this Chapter other than a power to report to the Minister.

A delegate must, upon request by a person affected by the exercise of any power delegated to the delegate, produce the instrument of delegation, or a copy of the instrument, for inspection by the person.

sections.source_quote: The Ombudsman may delegate: (a) to an APS employee responsible to the Ombudsman; or (b) to a person having similar oversight functions to the Ombudsman under the law of a State or Territory or to an employee responsible to that person, all or any of the Ombudsman's powers under this Chapter other than a power to report to the Minister. A delegate must, upon request by a person affected by the exercise of any power delegated to the delegate, produce the instrument of delegation, or a copy of the instrument, for inspection by the person.
sections.relevance_reason: Delegation of powers affects data handling oversight
